entries.title: Monaco GP
entries.headline: Ferrari's big chance as FIA neuters Mercedes power advantage
entries.summary: Antonelli leads Russell by 43 points after four straight wins, but Monaco's tight corners and no Straight Mode zones could finally hand Ferrari their first victory of 2026.
entries.body:
Kimi Antonelli has won four races in a row now — including a dramatic Canadian GP where he and George Russell were scrapping hard for the lead before Russell's power unit gave up on Lap 30. That's extended Antonelli's championship lead to 43 points, and the kid's only 19. Russell bounced back in Canada by taking pole for both the Sprint and Grand Prix — beating Antonelli by exactly 0.068s each time — and won the Sprint, but the main race went south when the mechanical gremlins hit. McLaren meanwhile are looking to rebound after a costly strategic misstep in Canada that left them off the podium despite having the pace. Mercedes have won every Grand Prix this season but that streak's under serious threat here.

Ferrari's car has looked very strong in corners but lacking on straights, whereas Mercedes will potentially lose some of its power advantage in Monaco. Even better for Ferrari — the FIA has confirmed there will be no Straight Mode zones at Monaco this weekend, with both wings staying in high-downforce configuration throughout for safety reasons. Ferrari continued their strong start with Lewis Hamilton topping FP2 from Charles Leclerc, and qualifying on Saturday is absolutely everything here because overtaking is nearly impossible. Leclerc could well take his fourth pole in Monaco, while Hamilton will be buoyed by a best-ever finish in red, with second last time out in Canada. If either Ferrari nails it on Saturday, don't be surprised if Mercedes finally drop their first Grand Prix of 2026.

entries.title: Barcelona-Cataluyna GP
entries.headline: Five in a Row — Can Anyone Stop Antonelli Before Barcelona?
entries.summary: Kimi Antonelli arrives in Barcelona with five straight wins and a 66-point championship lead, but the Circuit de Catalunya is a proper test for every part of the car — and Ferrari and McLaren both have a point to prove.
entries.body:
Monaco was another Antonelli masterclass, even if the chaos around him made it look easier than it was. Verstappen stalled and retired on lap 1, Norris broke down for the second race in a row, Leclerc binned it at the same corner as Stroll during the red flag restart, and Russell had an absolute nightmare — two penalties, a drive-through for not serving one properly, and finished 12th having started the weekend looking like the only man who could challenge his teammate. He's now 66 points behind Antonelli in the standings, Hamilton has leapfrogged him into second, and the championship is starting to look like a one-horse race. Barcelona can't come soon enough for everyone trying to close the gap.
The good news for the rest of the field is that Catalunya actually rewards a well-rounded car rather than one that's just fast in a straight line or brilliant in slow corners. It's a mix of everything — high speed, technical sections, long corners that eat rear tyres — and it's one of the few tracks where you can actually overtake, which means we might get a proper race rather than a Monaco-style procession. Teams typically bring big upgrades here because they know the data from Catalunya tells you a lot about where your car really stands. Ferrari will be desperate to put a proper challenge together after Leclerc's Monaco heartbreak, and McLaren need to find out why their car keeps breaking down. Antonelli won't be easy to beat — he never is right now — but Barcelona is where we'll find out whether anyone has genuinely closed the gap, or whether this title is already done.
